tool_calls.name: get_graph_schema
tool_calls.result_preview:
# AAS Graph Schema

All nodes also carry the label `GraphNode`.

## Core Nodes

### AssetAdministrationShell
The top-level AAS entity.
- Labels: AssetAdministrationShell, Identifiable, Referable, Refe

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})
      -[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(loc:Property)
WHERE loc.idShort = 'hall' OR loc.idShort = 'location'
MATCH (aas)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN aas.idShort AS device, aas.id AS aasId, asset.globalAssetId, loc.idShort, loc.value AS hall

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall3:facilityinformation'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.category, el.value, el.entityType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm2:Submodel)
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm2.idShort, sm2.id

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall3:hierarchicalstructures'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, el.category, el.entityType

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall3:hierarchicalstructures'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(e:Entity)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(a:Asset)
RETURN e.idShort AS device, a.globalAssetId AS assetId, e.entityType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
WHERE asset.globalAssetId IN ['urn:asset:ur20:001', 'urn:asset:mir250:002', 'urn:asset:ur3e:001', 'urn:asset:mir250:001']
RETURN aas.idShort AS deviceName, aas.id AS aasId, asset.globalAssetId AS assetId

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WITH aas, sm
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort AS asset_idShort, aas.id AS asset_id, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, sm.id AS submodel_id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId
ORDER BY aas.idShort

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(e:SubmodelElement)
RETURN e.idShort AS element_idShort, e.category AS category, e.modelType AS modelType
ORDER BY element_idShort

tool_calls.name: get_graph_schema
tool_calls.result_preview:
# AAS Graph Schema

All nodes also carry the label `GraphNode`.

## Core Nodes

### AssetAdministrationShell
The top-level AAS entity.
- Labels: AssetAdministrationShell, Identifiable, Referable, Refe

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, sm.idShort, sm.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall3:hierarchicalstructures'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(e:Entity)
RETURN e.idShort, e.entityType

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall3:hierarchicalstructures'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(parent:Entity)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(child:Entity)
MATCH (child)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN parent.idShort AS parent, child.idShort AS device, asset.globalAssetId AS assetId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
WHERE asset.globalAssetId IN ['urn:asset:mir250:001', 'urn:asset:ur3e:001', 'urn:asset:mir250:002', 'urn:asset:ur20:001']
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, asset.globalAssetId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(hallProp:Property)
WHERE hallProp.idShort = 'Hall' OR hallProp.idShort = 'hall'
RETURN sm.idShort, hallProp.idShort, hallProp.value

args.name: FacilityInformation

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN sm.idShort, el.idShort, labels(el), el.value

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/dfki/SubmodelTemplate/FacilityInformation/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
OPTIONAL MATCH (el)-[:HAS_VALUE]->(ls:LangString)
RETURN sm.idShort, el.idShort, el.value, ls.text

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(e:Entity)
OPTIONAL MATCH (e)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN e.idShort, e.entityType, asset.globalAssetId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: $idShort})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property)
RETURN p.idShort, p.value

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
WHERE asset.globalAssetId IN ['urn:asset:mir250:001', 'urn:asset:ur3e:001', 'urn:asset:mir250:002', 'urn:asset:ur20:001']
OPTIONAL MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, sm.idShort, sc.id

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ['MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002', 'UR3e_001', 'UR20_001']
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: $idShort})
OPTIONAL MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_DESCRIPTION]->(desc:LangString)
RETURN aas.idShort, desc.language, desc.text

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ['MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002', 'UR3e_001', 'UR20_001']
OPTIONAL MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_DESCRIPTION]->(desc:LangString {language: 'en'})
RETURN aas.idShort, desc.text AS description

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(e:SubmodelElement)
RETURN e.idShort, e.modelType
LIMIT 50

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/HierarchicalStructures/1/1/Submodel'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(ent:Entity)
OPTIONAL MATCH (ent)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN aas.idShort AS hallName, ent.idShort AS entityName, ent.entityType, asset.globalAssetId AS assetId, asset.assetKind

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(node:SubmodelElement)
RETURN aas.idShort, sm.idShort, node.idShort, labels(node)

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(ent:Entity)
OPTIONAL MATCH (ent)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN ent.idShort AS deviceName, ent.entityType, asset.globalAssetId AS assetId, asset.assetKind

WHERE asset.globalAssetId IN ['urn:asset:ur20:001', 'urn:asset:mir250:002', 'urn:asset:ur3e:001', 'urn:asset:mir250:001']
MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property)
RETURN asset.globalAssetId AS assetId, p.idShort, p.value, p.valueType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
WHERE asset.globalAssetId IN ['urn:asset:ur20:001', 'urn:asset:mir250:002', 'urn:asset:ur3e:001', 'urn:asset:mir250:001']
MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'CapabilityDescription'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property)
RETURN asset.globalAssetId AS assetId, p.idShort, p.value, p.valueType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
WHERE asset.globalAssetId IN ['urn:asset:ur20:001', 'urn:asset:mir250:002', 'urn:asset:ur3e:001', 'urn:asset:mir250:001']

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'})

RETURN aas.idShort AS device, aas.id AS aasId, sm.idShort AS smIdShort, sm.id AS smId

MATCH (e)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN e.idShort AS entityName, e.entityType, asset.globalAssetId AS assetId, aas.idShort AS hall

args.name: FacilityInformation

args.cypher:

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property {idShort: 'PrimaryUse'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(mlp:MultiLanguageProperty {idShort: 'PrimaryUse'})-[:HAS_VALUE]->(ls:LangString)
RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, ls.text AS primaryUse

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId

args.name: Hierarchical Structures enabling Bills of Material

args.cypher:

MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(node:Entity)
WHERE 'Entity' IN labels(node)
RETURN node.idShort, node.entityType
LIMIT 50

MATCH (sm:Submodel {idShort: 'HierarchicalStructures'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(parent:Entity {idShort: 'EntryNode'})
MATCH (parent)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(child:Entity)
WHERE 'Entity' IN labels(child) AND child.idShort <> 'EntryNode'
OPTIONAL MATCH (child)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
OPTIONAL MATCH (child)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(prop:Property)
WITH child, asset, collect(prop) AS props
RETURN child.idShort, asset.globalAssetId, [p IN props | {idShort: p.idShort, value: p.value}] AS properties

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: 'urn:aas:hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})

LIMIT 10

tool_calls.name: get_graph_schema
tool_calls.result_preview:
# AAS Graph Schema

All nodes also carry the label `GraphNode`.

## Core Nodes

### AssetAdministrationShell
The top-level AAS entity.
- Labels: AssetAdministrationShell, Identifiable, Referable, Refe

args.cypher:
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(a:Asset)
RETURN aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, aas.id AS aas_id, a.globalAssetId, a.assetKind
LIMIT 20

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN sm.idShort, sm.id, sc.id AS templateSemanticId

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall3:hierarchicalstructures'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, labels(el) AS types, el.entityType, el.value
LIMIT 30

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall3:hierarchicalstructures'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(parent:Entity {idShort: 'EntryNode'})
MATCH (parent)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(device:Entity)
MATCH (device)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset)
RETURN device.idShort AS device, aas.idShort AS aas_idShort, aas.id AS aas_id, asset.globalAssetId
ORDER BY device.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(a:Asset)
WHERE aas.idShort IN ['MiR250_001', 'MiR250_002', 'UR20_001', 'UR3e_001']
OPTIONAL MATCH (aas)-[:DERIVED_FROM]->(typeAas:AssetAdministrationShell)
MATCH (aas)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort AS device, aas.id AS aas_id, typeAas.idShort AS type_shell, sm.idShort AS submodel_idShort, sc.id AS templateSemanticId
ORDER BY device

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {id: $aasId})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})
      -[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property)
RETURN p.idShort, p.value, p.valueType

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall3:facilityinformation'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, labels(el) AS types, el.entityType, el.value
LIMIT 30

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:hall3:hierarchicalstructures'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(parent:Entity {idShort: 'EntryNode'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(device:Entity)
WHERE device.idShort <> 'EntryNode'
MATCH (device)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN device.idShort AS device_idShort, asset.globalAssetId AS asset_globalAssetId, asset.assetKind

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})
      -[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property)
WITH aas.idShort AS device_idShort, aas.id AS aas_id, 
     collect({idShort: p.idShort, value: p.value}) AS props
RETURN device_idShort, aas_id, props

MATCH (instance:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:DERIVED_FROM]->(type:AssetAdministrationShell)

MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*0..5]->(el:SubmodelElement)

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/HierarchicalStructures/1/1'})

result.response:
I'll help you identify the autonomous transport robots and their locations. Let me start by gathering the necessary schema and template information, then query the graph.

















I see the **Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles** template — that's the key one for AGVs. Now let me query for all AAS shells that have submodels with this semanticId (indicating AGV/autonomous transport robots), and also look for location data.









Good — I found two **type** shells (MiR100 and MiR250). Now I need to find the **instance** shells (the actual robots on the floor). These will be derived from the type shells via `DERIVED_FROM`.









Found 3 instance robots: **MiR100_001**, **MiR250_001**, and **MiR250_002**. Now let me find their locations. I'll check for Asset Location submodels and also look at the asset information.









I notice the instance shells (MiR100_001, MiR250_001, MiR250_002) only have a Nameplate submodel — no dedicated location submodel. Let me check if there's an Asset Location submodel template in the graph, and also look at the HierarchicalStructures submodels on the hall shells, since location might be tracked there.

















Now let me check if any of the instance shells have an AssetLocation submodel, and also look at the HierarchicalStructures to see how robots are assigned to halls/locations.









No Asset Location submodels exist. Let me check the Hall shells' HierarchicalStructures — the robots might be listed there with their hall locations. Let me also look at the Nameplate submodels on the instance shells for any location clues.









Excellent! The HierarchicalStructures submodels on the hall shells show the robot locations:
- **Hall3**: MiR250_001, MiR250_002
- **Hall4**: MiR100_001

Let me verify the model types of these Entity elements to confirm they represent the robots.









Now let me also check the nameplates for additional details (model info, manufacturer) on the instance shells.

























Here's the complete summary:

---

## Autonomous Transport Robots in the Factory

We have **3 autonomous mobile robots (AMRs)**, all manufactured by **MiR** (Mobile Industrial Robots), tracked via the IDTA *Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles* template.

| Robot | Model | Serial Number | Built | Location |
|-------|-------|---------------|-------|----------|
| **MiR100_001** | MiR100 | MIR100-2020-001 | 2020-04-12 | **Hall 4** |
| **MiR250_001** | MiR250 | MIR250-2023-001 | 2023-08-15 | **Hall 3** |
| **MiR250_002** | MiR250 | MIR250-2023-002 | 2023-09-20 | **Hall 3** |

**Key details:**
- **MiR100_001** — the older unit (2020), currently assigned to **Hall 4** alongside robot CRX10iA_001
- **MiR250_001 & MiR250_002** — newer units (2023), both assigned to **Hall 3** alongside UR20_001 and UR3e_001

**Note:** None of the instance shells currently have an *Asset Location* submodel (IDTA `smt/assetlocation`), so the location data comes from the *HierarchicalStructures* submodels on the Hall AAS shells, which list each asset as an Entity node under the respective hall.

ORDER BY aas.idShort, sm.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)

records.query: Which autonomous transport robots do we have and where are they located?

result.query: Which autonomous transport robots do we have and where are they located?
result.response:
I'll start by finding all autonomous transport robots in the system. Let me first check the graph schema and then query for the assets.









Now let me get the templates index to understand which submodel templates might identify autonomous transport robots, and query for all shells and their assets.









I found a relevant template: **"Technical Data for Automated Guided Vehicles"** (semanticId: `https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0`). Let me now query for all AAS shells that have submodels with this semantic ID — these are the autonomous transport robots (AGVs).









These are type shells (no instance shells). Let me also check for instance shells — autonomous transport robots that are actual deployed assets, which would be derived from these type shells.









Three instance robots found. Now let me get their location information by checking their submodels — likely the "Data Model for Asset Location" template.









The instance shells don't have the AGV technical data submodel or a location submodel directly. Let me check their type shells for location data, and also look for any location-related submodels more broadly.









The location submodel isn't present on any robot. Let me check the `FacilityInformation` submodel on the hall shells to see where the robots might be located, and also look at the `HierarchicalStructures` submodel which may contain asset-to-location relationships.









Good — there are Hall3 and Hall4 facility shells with HierarchicalStructures submodels. Let me check those for the robot locations.









The schema notes that trailing `/Submodel` may need stripping. Let me check what semantic IDs actually exist in the graph for the HierarchicalStructures submodel.









The ID is correct. Let me check the HierarchicalStructures submodel elements more broadly.









I can see the entities in Hall3's HierarchicalStructures. Let me now check Hall4 too, and get the asset references for each entity.









MiR250_001 and MiR250_002 are in Hall3. Let me also check Hall4 for MiR100_001.









Now let me get the model types (MiR100 vs MiR250) and a brief description of each robot model from the type shells.

















Here's a complete summary of all autonomous transport robots in the system:

---

## Autonomous Transport Robots (AGVs)

We have **3 deployed AGVs** across **2 MiR models**:

| Robot | Model | Hall | Load | Speed | Key Specs |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MiR100_001** | MiR100 (100 kg) | **Hall 4** | 100 kg | 1.0 m/s (loaded) / 1.5 m/s (empty) | 890×580×352 mm, 70 kg, Laser SLAM, IP21 |
| **MiR250_001** | MiR250 (250 kg) | **Hall 3** | 250 kg | 1.5 m/s (loaded) / 2.0 m/s (empty) | 1350×910×322 mm, 215 kg, Laser SLAM, IP52 |
| **MiR250_002** | MiR250 (250 kg) | **Hall 3** | 250 kg | 1.5 m/s (loaded) / 2.0 m/s (empty) | 1350×910×322 mm, 215 kg, Laser SLAM, IP52 |

### Summary
- **Hall 3** has 2× MiR250 robots (higher payload, larger footprint, IP52 rated)
- **Hall 4** has 1× MiR100 robot (lighter payload, smaller, IP21 rated)
- All three use **Laser SLAM** for localization and **differential drive** kinematics
- All navigate **free navigation** paths (not fixed-track)
- None are rated for outdoor use
- Runtime: MiR100 ~10 h, MiR250 ~15 h

*(Data from the graph: HierarchicalStructures submodels on Hall3/Hall4 facility shells and TechnicalDataAGV submodels on the MiR type shells.)*

tool_calls.name: get_graph_schema
tool_calls.result_preview:
# AAS Graph Schema

All nodes also carry the label `GraphNode`.

## Core Nodes

### AssetAdministrationShell
The top-level AAS entity.
- Labels: AssetAdministrationShell, Identifiable, Referable, Refe

args.cypher:
MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'})
MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm)
MATCH (aas)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot_name, aas.id AS aas_id, asset.globalAssetId AS asset_id
ORDER BY aas.idShort

MATCH (instance:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:DERIVED_FROM]->(type:AssetAdministrationShell)
WHERE type.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:type', 'urn:aas:mir250:type']
RETURN instance.idShort AS instance_name, instance.id AS instance_aas_id, type.idShort AS type_name
ORDER BY instance.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE aas.id IN ['urn:aas:mir100:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:001', 'urn:aas:mir250:002']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot, sm.idShort AS submodel, sc.id AS template_id
ORDER BY aas.idShort, sm.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sc.id = 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/smt/assetlocation/1/0'
RETURN aas.idShort AS robot, sm.idShort AS submodel, sc.id AS template_id
ORDER BY aas.idShort

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE hall.idShort IN ['Hall3', 'Hall4']
OPTIONAL MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
RETURN hall.idShort AS hall, sm.idShort AS submodel, sc.id AS template_id
ORDER BY hall.idShort, sm.idShort

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE hall.idShort IN ['Hall3', 'Hall4']
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/HierarchicalStructures/1/1/Submodel'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)
OPTIONAL MATCH (entity)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN hall.idShort AS hall, entity.idShort AS entity_name, asset.globalAssetId AS asset_id
ORDER BY hall.idShort, entity.idShort

MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(sc:SemanticConcept)
WHERE sm.idShort IN ['HierarchicalStructures']
RETURN DISTINCT sc.id AS semantic_id

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*0..]->(el:SubmodelElement)
RETURN el.idShort, labels(el)
ORDER BY el.idShort

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall3'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN hall.idShort AS hall, entity.idShort AS entity_name, asset.globalAssetId AS asset_id, labels(entity) AS entity_labels
ORDER BY entity.idShort

MATCH (hall:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'Hall4'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'HierarchicalStructures'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(entity:Entity)-[:REPRESENTS_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)
RETURN hall.idShort AS hall, entity.idShort AS entity_name, asset.globalAssetId AS asset_id
ORDER BY entity.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR100_Type'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'TechnicalDataAGV'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property)
RETURN p.idShort AS prop, p.value AS val
ORDER BY p.idShort

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR250_Type'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
WHERE sm.idShort = 'TechnicalDataAGV'
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(p:Property)
RETURN p.idShort AS prop, p.value AS val
ORDER BY p.idShort

Good. The autonomous transport robots are the **MiR** models (MiR100 and MiR250). I see instance shells for MiR100_001, MiR250_001, and MiR250_002. Let me also check the HierarchicalStructures submodels to find where they're placed.

Now let me get the Technical Data AGV info for the MiR100 and MiR250 type shells to know their model capabilities.

















I have all the data needed. Here's the summary:

**Autonomous Transport Robots — 3 units across 2 halls:**

| # | Model | Serial Number | Location | Key Specs |
|---|-------|--------------|----------|-----------|
| 1 | **MiR100** | MIR100-2020-001 | **Hall 4** | Max load 100 kg · 890×580×352 mm · Speed 1.0 m/s (loaded) / 1.5 m/s (empty) · 10 h runtime · Laser SLAM, free navigation |
| 2 | **MiR250** | MIR250-2023-001 | **Hall 3** | Max load 250 kg · 1350×910×322 mm · Speed 1.5 m/s (loaded) / 2.0 m/s (empty) · 15 h runtime · Laser SLAM, free navigation |

**Model overview:**
- **MiR100** — lighter carrier (70 kg), suitable for smaller loads in Hall 4.
- **MiR250** — heavier carrier (215 kg), higher load capacity, located in Hall 3 (two units).

Both use **free navigation with Laser SLAM** localization (no tracks or magnetic strips needed), differential drive kinematics, and are indoor-only (not suitable for outdoor use). The MiR250 has a higher IP rating (IP52 vs IP21) for better dust/water protection.

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell {idShort: 'MiR250_001'})-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel {idShort: 'Nameplate'})

args.cypher:
MATCH (sm:Submodel)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'})

RETURN aas.idShort, aas.id, asset.globalAssetId AS assetGlobalAssetId

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'})
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(generic:SubmodelElementCollection {idShort: 'SpecificDescriptions'})
MATCH (generic)-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(techParams:SubmodelElementCollection {idShort: 'TechnicalParameters'})
MATCH (techParams)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(maxLoad:Property {idShort: 'MaxLoadMass'})-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: '0173-1#02-ABJ258#001'})
RETURN aas.idShort AS robotType, maxLoad.value AS maxLoadMass, maxLoad.valueType AS valueType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'})
RETURN aas.idShort AS aasIdShort, aas.id AS aasId, sm.idShort AS smIdShort, sm.id AS smId

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:mir100:type:technicaldataagv'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(coll:SubmodelElementCollection {idShort: 'TechnicalParameters'})
MATCH (coll)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(p:Property)
RETURN p.idShort AS elementName, p.value AS value, p.valueType AS valueType, labels(p) AS types

MATCH (sm:Submodel {id: 'urn:submodel:mir250:type:technicaldataagv'})-[:HAS_ELEMENT*]->(coll:SubmodelElementCollection {idShort: 'TechnicalParameters'})
MATCH (coll)-[:HAS_ELEMENT]->(p:Property {idShort: 'MaxLoadMass'})
RETURN p.idShort AS elementName, p.value AS value, p.valueType AS valueType

MATCH (aas:AssetAdministrationShell)-[:HAS_SUBMODEL]->(sm:Submodel)
MATCH (sm)-[:HAS_SEMANTIC_ID]->(:SemanticConcept {id: 'https://admin-shell.io/idta/SubmodelTemplate/technicaldataagv/1/0'})
OPTIONAL MATCH (aas)-[:DERIVED_FROM]->(parent:AssetAdministrationShell)
OPTIONAL MATCH (aas)-[:MANAGES_ASSET]->(asset:Asset)

| MiR100 | 100 kg |
| **MiR250** | **250 kg** |

The MiR250 is the only transport robot in the system with a payload capacity exceeding 200 kg (250 kg). It's available as two instances: **MiR250_001** and **MiR250_002**.

The MiR100, by comparison, has a max load of only 100 kg and does not meet your requirement.

*Source: TechnicalDataAGV submodel (TechnicalParameters → MaxLoadMass) for both type shells.*

records.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?

result.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?
result.response:
I'll help you find information about emergency stops in the MiR 250 manual. Let me first locate the relevant AAS and submodel, then search the documentation.

















No exact `idShort` match. Let me search more broadly for MiR-related assets.









I found MiR 250 AAS instances and a type shell. Let me check which submodels contain documentation (PDFs) — following VDI 2770, the type shell is the right place for manuals.









The `HandoverDocumentation` submodel likely contains the PDF manual. Let me find the PDF file reference and then search it.









Found the PDF. Now let me search the MiR 250 manual for emergency stop information.









The MiR 250 manual has substantial emergency stop information. Here's what it says:

---

## Emergency Stops on the MiR 250

### What Emergency Stop Does
When an Emergency Stop button is physically pressed, the robot immediately cuts power to all moving parts and the top application via internal safety contactors. You can hear the safety contactors click. The status light turns **red**, and the robot cannot be moved or sent on missions until the Emergency Stop is released and the **Resume button** is pressed.

- **[MiR250 manual p. 84](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=84)**

### Recovery
1. Release the Emergency Stop button
2. Press the Resume button (it begins flashing blue after release)
3. The robot immediately returns to an operating state

- **[MiR250 manual p. 210](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=210)**

### Hardware
- The MiR250 has **one built-in Emergency Stop button**
- Additional Emergency Stop buttons can be connected via the **electrical interface**
- The Emergency Stop circuit runs through the **Auxiliary Emergency Stop interface** and connects to the top module
- Multiple buttons can be connected in series to the circuit
- When the circuit is broken → robot enters Emergency Stop

- **[MiR250 manual p. 86](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=86)**

### Electrical Interface Details
- Pins 2 & 3 deliver 24 V from the safety PLC
- Pins 4 & 5 connect to the Emergency Stop circuit inputs of the safety PLC
- 24 V must be present on pins 4 & 5 for the robot to operate
- Pushing an E-Stop button causes both pins to drop to 0 V → robot enters Emergency Stop
- If the input pins don't match, the robot enters **Protective Stop** instead

- **[MiR250 manual p. 95](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=95)**

### Shared Emergency Stop
There is a shared E-Stop circuit between the robot and the top module, so pressing E-Stop on one triggers the other into Emergency Stop as well.

- **[MiR250 manual p. 87](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=87)**

### Troubleshooting
The robot computer connects to the safety PLC via Ethernet. The PLC sends all its input statuses to the robot computer, which helps identify which part of the safety system caused a Protective or Emergency Stop.

- **[MiR250 manual p. 99](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=99)**

### Emergency Stop vs. Protective Stop
| | Emergency Stop | Protective Stop |
|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | Physical E-Stop button pressed | Safety scanner, fault detection, mode change, etc. |
| **Status light** | Red | Red |
| **Recovery** | Release button → press Resume | Press Resume after fault is cleared |
| **Power cut** | Yes (safety contactors open) | No (robot still powered) |

---

All information comes from the MiR250 operator manual ingested into the Type AAS Handover Documentation submodel.

records.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?

result.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?
result.response:
I'll help you find information about emergency stops in the MiR 250 manual. First, I need to locate the MiR 250 AAS and its associated documentation.

















Let me first find the MiR 250 AAS by listing available shells.









I found the MiR 250 shells. Documentation is typically on the Type shell, so let me find the PDF documents in the MiR 250 Type AAS.









Found the MiR 250 manual PDF. Now let me search it for emergency stop information.









The results aren't very specific to emergency stop procedures. Let me search with more targeted terms from the manual's safety section.









I found some relevant hits. Let me get more specific content about emergency stop locations and reset procedures.









Let me now search specifically for the reset procedure and the emergency stop scanner covers.









Let me search more specifically for the scanner covers and protective/emergency stop information.









Let me search for the section on Emergency stop and Protective stop specifically — the manual section around page 85-86.









Now let me search for the specific section about Emergency stop buttons and the scanner covers triggering emergency stops.









Let me search specifically for the Emergency stop section and how to identify/resolve emergency stop triggers.









Let me get the specific page about Emergency stop buttons location and the section on page 85-86.









Let me search for the section that describes the Emergency stop buttons themselves and the scanner covers that can trigger them.









I now have enough information to compile a comprehensive answer about emergency stops in the MiR 250 manual. Let me summarize what I found.

---

## Emergency Stops — MiR 250 Operator Manual

Based on the MiR 250 User Guide (v.1.1, 07/2020), here's what the manual says about emergency stops:

### ⚠️ Important Notice (p. 85)
> Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.

### How Emergency Stops Work (p. 86)
The MiR 250 has an **Emergency stop circuit** that:
- Goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and connects to the top module
- Can have **any number of Emergency stop buttons** connected to the circuit
- When the circuit is **broken**, the robot goes into **Emergency stop**

### Shared Emergency Stop (p. 87, 97)
- The **Shared emergency stop interface** controls the Emergency stop state between the robot and a top module
- Has **two inputs** for bringing the robot into Emergency stop and **two outputs** for signaling when the robot is in Emergency stop
- When outputs deliver **24 V**, the robot can operate; when either or both deliver **0 V**, the robot enters Emergency stop
- If pins are unequally set for **more than 3 seconds**, the safety PLC registers an error and the robot must be **restarted**

### Identifying the Cause (p. 99)
When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a safety component:
- Sign in to the **robot interface**
- Go to **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**
- This shows **exactly which component** is causing the issue

### Troubleshooting Hardware Errors (p. 209)
For hardware-related emergency stops:
1. **Turn the robot off and then on again** — resets the robot components
2. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released**
3. **Check for physical damage** — cracks, dents, severe scratches, or contamination (dust, dirt, grease) — especially on **3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels**
4. Go to **Monitoring > Hardware health** to see which component is failing

### Maintenance — Scanner Covers (p. 178)
The manual specifically calls out checking scanner covers:
> In the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop**, see if **Front scanner cover** and **Back scanner cover** is **Clean**.

### Color Indication (p. 18)
- **Blinking blue**: The robot is waiting for a user action — clear the Emergency stop state or acknowledge the change of operating mode

### Auxiliary Safety Interfaces (p. 205)
Both **Safety A** and **Safety B** interfaces have dedicated pins for:
- **Shared emergency stop in** — Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- **Shared E-Stop out** — Active when the robot is in emergency stop
- **Safeguarded stop** — Can trigger Protective stop or Emergency stop depending on configuration

### Safety Warnings (p. 36)
> Personnel operating near the robot must be informed on how to engage the robot's Emergency stop function in emergency situations.

---

**Sources:**
- [MiR250 manual p. 85 — NOTICE](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)
- [MiR250 manual p. 86 — Emergency stop circuit](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=86)
- [MiR250 manual p. 97 — Shared emergency stop](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=97)
- [MiR250 manual p. 99 — Common issues](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=99)
- [MiR250 manual p. 178 — Maintenance tasks](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=178)

result.final_answer:
I now have enough information to compile a comprehensive answer about emergency stops in the MiR 250 manual. Let me summarize what I found.

---

## Emergency Stops — MiR 250 Operator Manual

Based on the MiR 250 User Guide (v.1.1, 07/2020), here's what the manual says about emergency stops:

### ⚠️ Important Notice (p. 85)
> Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.

### How Emergency Stops Work (p. 86)
The MiR 250 has an **Emergency stop circuit** that:
- Goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and connects to the top module
- Can have **any number of Emergency stop buttons** connected to the circuit
- When the circuit is **broken**, the robot goes into **Emergency stop**

### Shared Emergency Stop (p. 87, 97)
- The **Shared emergency stop interface** controls the Emergency stop state between the robot and a top module
- Has **two inputs** for bringing the robot into Emergency stop and **two outputs** for signaling when the robot is in Emergency stop
- When outputs deliver **24 V**, the robot can operate; when either or both deliver **0 V**, the robot enters Emergency stop
- If pins are unequally set for **more than 3 seconds**, the safety PLC registers an error and the robot must be **restarted**

### Identifying the Cause (p. 99)
When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a safety component:
- Sign in to the **robot interface**
- Go to **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**
- This shows **exactly which component** is causing the issue

### Troubleshooting Hardware Errors (p. 209)
For hardware-related emergency stops:
1. **Turn the robot off and then on again** — resets the robot components
2. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released**
3. **Check for physical damage** — cracks, dents, severe scratches, or contamination (dust, dirt, grease) — especially on **3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels**
4. Go to **Monitoring > Hardware health** to see which component is failing

### Maintenance — Scanner Covers (p. 178)
The manual specifically calls out checking scanner covers:
> In the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop**, see if **Front scanner cover** and **Back scanner cover** is **Clean**.

### Color Indication (p. 18)
- **Blinking blue**: The robot is waiting for a user action — clear the Emergency stop state or acknowledge the change of operating mode

### Auxiliary Safety Interfaces (p. 205)
Both **Safety A** and **Safety B** interfaces have dedicated pins for:
- **Shared emergency stop in** — Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop
- **Shared E-Stop out** — Active when the robot is in emergency stop
- **Safeguarded stop** — Can trigger Protective stop or Emergency stop depending on configuration

### Safety Warnings (p. 36)
> Personnel operating near the robot must be informed on how to engage the robot's Emergency stop function in emergency situations.

---

**Sources:**
- [MiR250 manual p. 85 — NOTICE](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)
- [MiR250 manual p. 86 — Emergency stop circuit](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=86)
- [MiR250 manual p. 97 — Shared emergency stop](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=97)

I now have comprehensive information. Here's what the MiR250 operator manual says about emergency stops:

---

## MiR250 — Emergency Stop Summary

### Usage Warning
> **NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

### Types of Stop (p. 83)
The MiR250 has four stopped states:
- **Operational stop**
- **Protective stop** — monitored by safety PLC
- **Emergency stop** — monitored by safety PLC
- **Manual stop** — monitored by safety PLC

### Emergency Stop Circuit (p. 94)
- The E-stop circuit goes through the **Auxiliary safety function interface** (interfaces A and B) on the top module.
- The safety PLC outputs a **24 V signal** that passes through all E-stop buttons, then to two input pins.
- **Both input pins must receive 24 V** for the robot to operate.
- Pressing any E-stop button **breaks the circuit** → both inputs go to **0 V** → robot enters Emergency stop.
- If the circuit is wired incorrectly so the two input signals **differ**, the robot enters **Protective stop** until the circuit is fixed.

### Shared Emergency Stop (p. 98)
- Pins 3 (input) and 6 (output) on both interfaces A and B are used for shared E-stop.
- If the robot receives **0 V** from the shared E-stop interface → enters Emergency stop.
- If the two inputs are **unequal** → enters Emergency stop.

### Auxiliary Interface Pinout (p. 205)

**Interface A:**
| Pin | Signal | Type | Description |
|-----|--------|------|-------------|
| 3 | Shared emergency stop in 1 | Input | Inactive = robot goes into E-stop |
| 6 | Shared emergency stop out 1 | Output | Inactive when robot is in E-stop |

**Interface B:**
| Pin | Signal | Type | Description |
|-----|--------|------|-------------|
| 3 | Shared emergency stop in 2 | Input | Inactive = robot goes into E-stop |
| 6 | Shared E-Stop out 2 | Output | Active when robot is in E-stop |

### Identifying Which E-Stop Triggered (p. 99)
Go to the **robot interface** → **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system** to see exactly which safety component caused the stop.

### Status Indicator (p. 18)
- **Blinking blue** on the robot: waiting for user action — clear the Emergency stop state or acknowledge the operating mode change.

### Maintenance Check (p. 178)
Check under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop** whether the **Front scanner cover** and **Back scanner cover** are **Clean** — dirty covers can trigger E-stop faults.

---

**Bottom line:** If the MiR250 is in Emergency stop, check the robot interface under *Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system* to identify the triggering component, clear any physical obstructions (especially scanner covers), then reset via the robot interface. Use E-stops only in genuine emergencies.

I now have a good collection of relevant excerpts. Let me compile the findings from the MiR250 manual about emergency stops.

---

According to the **MiR250 Operator Manual** (rev. v.1.1, dated 07/2020), here is what the manual says about **Emergency Stops**:

## 1. What is the Emergency Stop?

The MiR250 has built-in **Emergency stop buttons** as part of its safety system. The manual states:

> *"Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use."*  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

## 2. How to identify an Emergency Stop

When the robot enters an emergency stop, the **status light blinks blue**:

> *"Blinking blue: The robot is waiting for a user action (clear the Emergency stop state, acknowledge the change of operating mode)."*  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 18](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=18)

## 3. Troubleshooting an Emergency Stop

From the **Common issues** section (7.21):

> *"When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a component in the safety system, you can identify exactly which component is causing the issue by signing in to the robot interface and viewing the errors displayed under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**."*  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 99](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=99)

From the **Hardware errors** section (16.2), the manual recommends:

1. **Turn the robot off and on again** — this resets the robot components and may resolve the issue.
2. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released.**
3. **Check the robot for physical damage** (cracks, dents, scratches) or contamination (dust, dirt, grease), especially on the 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels.
4. **Sign in to the robot interface** → **Monitoring > Hardware health** to identify which component is failing.
5. If an internal component is failing: turn off the robot, disconnect the battery, and have the commissioner visually check the internal component.
6. **Contact your distributor or MiR Technical Support** for further troubleshooting.  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 209](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209)

## 4. Maintenance: Checking the Emergency Stop System

The manual includes emergency stop checks in its **Regular maintenance** schedule:

> *"In the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop**, see if Front scanner cover and Back scanner cover is Clean."*  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 178](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=178)

## 5. External Safety Interfaces (Auxiliary Safety Functions)

The MiR250 has **Auxiliary safety function interfaces (A and B)** for integration with top modules. These include dedicated **Shared emergency stop in** and **Shared E-Stop out** signals:

- **Pin 3** (interface A & B): *Shared emergency stop in* — "Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop."
- **Pin 6** (interface A): *Shared emergency stop out* — "Inactive when the robot is in emergency stop."
- **Pin 6** (interface B): *Shared E-Stop out* — "Active when the robot is in emergency stop."  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 205](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=205)

---

**In summary:** The MiR250's emergency stop is a safety-critical function that halts the robot immediately when activated. To clear it, check that the E-stop button is released, review the **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system** panel for the specific fault, clean the scanner covers if needed, and if necessary power-cycle the robot. The manual strongly advises using E-stops only in genuine emergencies, not for routine stopping.

## 1. What is the Emergency Stop?

The MiR250 has built-in **Emergency stop buttons** as part of its safety system. The manual states:

> *"Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use."*  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

## 2. How to identify an Emergency Stop

When the robot enters an emergency stop, the **status light blinks blue**:

> *"Blinking blue: The robot is waiting for a user action (clear the Emergency stop state, acknowledge the change of operating mode)."*  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 18](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=18)

## 3. Troubleshooting an Emergency Stop

From the **Common issues** section (7.21):

> *"When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a component in the safety system, you can identify exactly which component is causing the issue by signing in to the robot interface and viewing the errors displayed under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**."*  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 99](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=99)

From the **Hardware errors** section (16.2), the manual recommends:

1. **Turn the robot off and on again** — this resets the robot components and may resolve the issue.
2. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released.**
3. **Check the robot for physical damage** (cracks, dents, scratches) or contamination (dust, dirt, grease), especially on the 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels.
4. **Sign in to the robot interface** → **Monitoring > Hardware health** to identify which component is failing.
5. If an internal component is failing: turn off the robot, disconnect the battery, and have the commissioner visually check the internal component.
6. **Contact your distributor or MiR Technical Support** for further troubleshooting.  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 209](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209)

## 4. Maintenance: Checking the Emergency Stop System

The manual includes emergency stop checks in its **Regular maintenance** schedule:

> *"In the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop**, see if Front scanner cover and Back scanner cover is Clean."*  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 178](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=178)

## 5. External Safety Interfaces (Auxiliary Safety Functions)

The MiR250 has **Auxiliary safety function interfaces (A and B)** for integration with top modules. These include dedicated **Shared emergency stop in** and **Shared E-Stop out** signals:

- **Pin 3** (interface A & B): *Shared emergency stop in* — "Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop."
- **Pin 6** (interface A): *Shared emergency stop out* — "Inactive when the robot is in emergency stop."
- **Pin 6** (interface B): *Shared E-Stop out* — "Active when the robot is in emergency stop."  
> — [MiR250 manual p. 205](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=205)

---

**In summary:** The MiR250's emergency stop is a safety-critical function that halts the robot immediately when activated. To clear it, check that the E-stop button is released, review the **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system** panel for the specific fault, clean the scanner covers if needed, and if necessary power-cycle the robot. The manual strongly advises using E-stops only in genuine emergencies, not for routine stopping.

RETURN sm.id AS submodelId, f.idShort, f.value

result.response:

The results touch on related topics but I need the most critical sections. Let me do one more targeted search specifically about the emergency stop state and status lights.









I now have a comprehensive picture from the MiR250 manual. Here's what it says about emergency stops:

---

## MiR250 Emergency Stop — Summary from the Manual

### ⚠️ Usage warning
> **NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.
> — [MiR250 manual p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

### Status light indication
- **Blinking blue** — The robot is waiting for a user action (clear the Emergency stop state, or acknowledge the change of operating mode).
> — [MiR250 manual p. 18](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=18)

### Troubleshooting hardware errors (including E-stop)
If the robot has an emergency stop fault:
1. **Turn the robot off and on again** — resets the robot components and may resolve the issue.
2. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released.**
3. **Inspect the robot** for physical damage (cracks, dents, scratches) or contamination (dust, dirt, grease), especially on the 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels.
4. **Log into the robot interface** → *Monitoring > Hardware Health* to see which component is failing.
5. If an internal component is failing: turn off the robot, disconnect the battery, and have the commissioner/operator visually check the component.
6. For further help, contact your distributor or MiR Technical Support.
> — [MiR250 manual p. 209](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209)

### Identifying which safety component triggered the stop
When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a safety system component:
- Sign in to the **robot interface** → *Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system* to see exactly which component caused the stop.
- Maintenance tasks also show under *Monitoring > Hardware Health > Emergency stop* — check if the **Front scanner cover** and **Back scanner cover** are clean.
> — [MiR250 manual p. 99](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=99)

### Shared emergency stop (external safety circuit)
MiR250 supports a **Shared emergency stop** via the Auxiliary safety function interfaces (A and B):
- Pins 3 are used for input, pins 6 for input of the Shared E-stop function.
- The robot outputs 24 V when **not** in Emergency stop; it outputs **0 V** when in Emergency stop (either from shared interface or unequal inputs).
> — [MiR250 manual p. 98](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=98)

### Key takeaway
To clear an Emergency stop: **release the E-stop button(s)** → **turn robot off/on** → check *Monitoring > Hardware Health* for any remaining faults → press **Resume** if in Protective stop.

records.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?

result.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?
result.response:
I'll help you find information about emergency stops in the MiR250 operator manual. Let me first discover the MiR250's AAS and locate its documentation.

















Found the MiR250 instance shells and a Type shell. Per VDI 2770, operator manual documentation typically lives on the **Type** shell. Let me discover its submodels and look for PDF documents.









The `HandoverDocumentation` submodel is the most likely place for the operator manual. Let me check for PDF files there.









Found the MiR250 PDF. Now let me search it for emergency stop information.









Good, I have some results. Let me also search more specifically for the safety system sections and E-stop reset/clear procedures.









Now let me also search for the safety stop modes and their distinctions, which is key to understanding emergency stops on the MiR250.









I now have a comprehensive picture. Here's what the MiR250 operator manual says about emergency stops:

---

## MiR250 Emergency Stop — Summary from the Operator Manual

### ⚠️ Key Warning
> **Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations.** They are not designed for frequent use.
> — [MiR250 manual p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

### Safety System Overview
The MiR250 safety system is designed to stop or slow the robot and its top module when personnel are at risk of injury. It is designed to **ISO 13849-1** and supports compliance with **EN 1525** and **ISO 3691-4**.

The robot recognizes two main stop modes:

| Mode | What happens | How to recover |
|---|---|---|
| **Protective stop** | Internal safety contactors switch off — top application and all moving parts lose power. You hear audible clicks. Status lights turn **red**. | Remove the cause (e.g., clear object from scanner field), then press the **Resume** button. The robot resumes its operating state. |
| **Emergency stop** | Immediate halt via E-stop button or external E-stop signal. | Release the E-stop button, then clear the E-stop state through the robot interface. |

### Status Light Indicator
- **Blinking blue**: The robot is waiting for a user action — specifically, to **clear the Emergency stop state** or acknowledge a change of operating mode.
> — [MiR250 manual p. 18](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=18)

### Troubleshooting an Emergency Stop
When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a safety system component issue:

1. **Identify the cause** — sign in to the robot interface and go to:
   `Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system`
   — [MiR250 manual p. 99](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=99)

2. **For hardware errors** (p. 209):
   - Turn the robot off and on again to reset components
   - **Check that the Emergency stop button is released**
   - Check for physical damage (cracks, dents, scratches) or contamination (dust, dirt, grease) on 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels
   - Go to `Monitoring > Hardware health` to see which component is failing
   - If an internal component fails, turn off the robot, disconnect the battery, and have the operator visually inspect it

3. **For scanner-related stops** (p. 84): Remove the object from the scanner's active protective field — the robot resumes after ~2 seconds.

### External E-Stop via Auxiliary Interfaces
The MiR250 has two auxiliary safety interfaces (A and B) with shared E-stop inputs:

- **Pin 3 (Safety A)**: "Shared emergency stop in input 1. **Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop.**"
- **Pin 3 (Safety B)**: "Shared emergency stop in input 2. Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop."

These can be wired to external E-stop circuits (e.g., from a top module or external safety system).

### Maintenance — E-Stop Related
As part of routine maintenance (p. 178):
> In the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop**, check if Front scanner cover and Back scanner cover is **Clean**.

---

**Source**: MiR250 operator manual (MiR250.pdf), Type AAS — `HandoverDocumentation` submodel. Pages referenced above are from the version dated 07/2020, revision v.1.1.

records.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?

result.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?
result.response:
I'll help you find information about emergency stops in the MiR 250 operator manual. First, I need to locate the MiR 250 AAS and its associated documentation.









Now let me find the MiR 250 AAS in the graph:









I can see the MiR250 assets. Now let me check the HandoverDocumentation submodel on the Type AAS for PDF files:









Found the PDF. Now let me search for emergency stop information in that document:









Good, I have relevant results. Let me get more specific information by searching for "emergency stop button" and "release" to find the full procedure:









Let me also search for more details on the emergency stop status light and troubleshooting:









Based on the MiR 250 operator manual, here's what it says about **emergency stops**:

---

## Emergency Stop — MiR 250 Manual Summary

### ⚠️ Usage Rule
> **NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are **not designed for frequent use**.
> — [MiR250 manual p. 85](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)

### Emergency Stop Circuit
- The emergency stop circuit goes through the **Auxiliary emergency stop interface** and connects to the top module.
- Any number of emergency stop buttons can be connected to the circuit.
- **When the circuit is broken, the robot goes into Emergency stop.**
> — [MiR250 manual p. 86](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=86)

### Status Light Indication
- **Blinking blue:** The robot is waiting for a user action — specifically to **clear the Emergency stop state** or acknowledge the change of operating mode.
> — [MiR250 manual p. 18](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=18)

### Troubleshooting / Recovery Steps
If the robot is in an emergency stop state, follow these steps:

1. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released.**
2. **Turn the robot off and then on again** — this resets the robot components and may resolve the issue.
3. **Check the robot for physical damage** — cracks, dents, severe scratches, or contamination (dust, dirt, grease), especially on the 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, and drive wheels.
4. **Sign in to the robot interface** and go to **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system** to identify exactly which component triggered the safety stop.
5. If an internal component is failing, **turn off the robot, disconnect the battery**, and have the commissioner/operator visually check the internal component.
6. For further troubleshooting, **contact your distributor** for MiR troubleshooting guides or MiR Technical Support.
> — [MiR250 manual pp. 99, 209](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209)

### Maintenance Check
- In the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Emergency stop**, check whether the **Front scanner cover** and **Back scanner cover** are listed as **Clean**.
> — [MiR250 manual p. 178](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=178)

---

**Key takeaway:** The emergency stop is a safety circuit — only use it in emergencies. If triggered, release the button, power-cycle the robot, inspect for damage, and use the robot interface to identify the specific fault before restarting operations.

records.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?

result.query: What does the operator manual of the mir 250 say about emergency stops?
result.response:
I'll help you find information about emergency stops in the MiR250 operator manual. Let me start by locating the MiR250 asset and its documentation.

















Let me list all assets to find the MiR250:









Found the MiR250 assets. There are two instance shells and one type shell. Documentation is typically on the **type shell**. Let me find the PDF documents linked to the MiR250 type shell:









Found the MiR250 PDF. Now let me search it for emergency stop information:









The initial results are somewhat scattered. Let me do a more targeted search specifically for the emergency stop section:









Let me get more specific results about the emergency stop section itself:









Let me get the most relevant page — the "Emergency stop button released" section on page 95:









Let me also check the safety system and E-stop related external interface details:









I now have enough information to provide a comprehensive answer. Here's what the MiR250 operator manual says about emergency stops:

---

## Emergency Stops on the MiR250 — According to the manual

The MiR250 operator manual covers emergency stops across several sections:

### 1. **Usage Warning** (p. 85)
> **NOTICE:** Only press the Emergency stop buttons in emergency situations. They are not designed for frequent use.

### 2. **Robot Status Indication** (p. 18)
When the robot has entered an emergency stop state, the status light will show:
- **Blinking blue** — The robot is waiting for a user action: clear the Emergency stop state or acknowledge the change of operating mode.

### 3. **Clearing / Resetting an Emergency Stop** (p. 209, §16.2 Hardware Errors)
If the robot is in an emergency stop fault state:
1. **Check that the Emergency stop button is released** — physically verify the E-stop button has been turned/pulled out.
2. Turn the robot **off and then on again** — this resets the robot components and may resolve the issue.
3. Check for **physical damage** (cracks, dents, severe scratches, contamination on 3D cameras, safety laser scanners, or drive wheels).
4. Sign into the robot interface → **Monitoring > Hardware health** to identify which component is failing.
5. If an internal component is failing, turn off the robot, disconnect the battery, and have the commissioner or operator visually inspect it.
6. Contact your distributor or MiR Technical Support for further troubleshooting.

### 4. **Diagnostic via Robot Interface** (p. 99, §7.21 Common Issues)
When the robot enters Protective or Emergency stop due to a safety system component, you can identify the exact cause:
- Sign into the robot interface
- Go to **Monitoring > Hardware Health > Safety system**
- The displayed errors tell you which specific component triggered the stop

### 5. **Maintenance Checks** (p. 178)
Regular maintenance includes checking the emergency stop system:
- In the robot interface under **Monitoring > Hardware health > Emergency stop**, verify that the Front scanner cover and Back scanner cover are clean.

### 6. **External Safety Interface** (p. 205, §15.6 — Auxiliary Safety Function Interface)
The MiR250 supports external emergency stop circuits through its Auxiliary safety function interfaces (A and B). Key pin functions related to emergency stop:

| Pin | Signal | Description |
|-----|--------|-------------|
| 3 | Shared emergency stop in 1 | **Input** — Inactive = robot goes into emergency stop |
| 6 | Shared emergency stop out 1 | **Output** — Inactive when the robot is in emergency stop |

This allows multiple MiR robots or external safety circuits to be wired into a shared emergency stop loop.

---

**Quick reference links to the PDF:**
- [p. 85 — Usage notice](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=85)
- [p. 95 — Emergency stop button released](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=95)
- [p. 99 — Common issues & diagnostics](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=99)
- [p. 205 — External safety interface pinout](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=205)
- [p. 209 — Hardware error troubleshooting](http://localhost:8091/submodels/dXJuOnN1Ym1vZGVsOm1pcjI1MDp0eXBlOmhhbmRvdmVyZG9jdW1lbnRhdGlvbg/submodel-elements/Documents%5B0%5D.DocumentVersions%5B0%5D.DigitalFiles%5B0%5D/attachment#page=209)

result.response:
